content.text: Real UI data is often hierarchical: categories with items, threads with replies, menus with submenus. React handles this naturally because
is just JavaScript — you can call it inside the callback of another
to produce nested JSX. The only rules are that every list needs
props and keys only need to be unique within the list they belong to — not globally.

attrs.alt: Nested list data structure mapped to nested JSX
attrs.title: Outer .map() over categories; inner .map() over each category's items

Extracting inner list to a component
is hard to read. Extract the inner list into its own component. This also gives the inner list its own named scope for
A common pattern is a flat list of items that you group by a field (e.g. by category, date, or status). Group the data first, then render each group with a heading:
(a string, unique per group) as the key. The inner list uses
Three levels of nesting are manageable when each level is extracted to its own component. Beyond three levels, consider whether the data model can be flattened instead:
When the depth is unknown at authoring time (file-system trees, comment threads, menus), use a recursive component. The component renders itself for each node's children:
renders itself, it works at any depth. The recursion terminates when a node has no
array or an empty one. React tracks each node by its
Keys in nested lists — the rules
Each list needs keys on its root element
React tracks which element is which across re-renders
Keys only unique within sibling list
item.id=11 can exist in two categories
React checks keys per parent, not globally
key goes on the outermost element returned by map
React needs to identify the list item, not a child
Extracted components do not receive key as a prop
key is consumed by React, not passed into the component
Grouped list with a count badge
Grouped lists often show an item count next to the group header. Derive it from the data rather than storing it separately:
with a grouped map produces a collapsible accordion. Track which groups are open in a
Flat data model vs nested data model
Nested lists are natural for tree data. But deeply nested state is hard to update immutably. Consider normalising the data for complex trees:
Natural, easy to read from API
Deep spreads needed to update one leaf
Flat map by id + childIds array
Extra lookup step to build render tree
Write mutating code; immer creates immutable copy
Adds dependency; magic mutations can surprise readers

nesting alone is not sufficient for interactive trees.
